title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 3
author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 3 attributed to the Viśvāmitra family
date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
summary: The Padapāṭha is the 'word-for-word' recitational text of the Ṛgveda in which each word is given in its isolated pausal form, dissolving the sandhi (euphonic combinations) of the continuous Saṃhitāpāṭha and resolving compounds. This file covers Maṇḍala 3, the 'family book' of the Viśvāmitra clan, whose hymns are addressed largely to Agni, Indra, and the Viśvedevas, and which contains the famous Gāyatrī mantra (RV 3.62.10). The Padapāṭha was created to preserve exact pronunciation, accent, and word boundaries across oral transmission.
history: The surviving Ṛgveda Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya (the Brahma and Vāyu Purāṇas name Vidagdha/Śākalya), and it forms the analytical basis for the Prātiśākhya and Pāṇinian grammar; the Maṇḍala 3 hymns are among the oldest 'family books' of the collection. The Ṛgveda's oral transmission via Saṃhitā- and Padapāṭha is recognized in UNESCO's Memory of the World register.

title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 4
author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 4 attributed to the Vāmadeva family
date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
summary: The word-for-word Padapāṭha text of Maṇḍala 4 of the Ṛgveda, the 'family book' of the Vāmadeva (Gautama) clan. Its hymns are addressed chiefly to Agni and Indra, with notable hymns to the Ṛbhus and to Uṣas; the Padapāṭha presents each word in isolated, sandhi-resolved pausal form for exact preservation of pronunciation and accent.
history: The Ṛgveda Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya and underpins the later grammatical and phonetic (Prātiśākhya) tradition. Maṇḍala 4 belongs to the older core of family books transmitted orally with extraordinary fidelity for over three millennia.

title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 5
author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 5 attributed to the Atri family
date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
summary: The Padapāṭha (word-for-word) recitational text of Maṇḍala 5 of the Ṛgveda, the 'family book' of the Atri clan. Its hymns honor primarily Agni, Indra, the Maruts, Mitra-Varuṇa, and the Viśvedevas; the Padapāṭha isolates every word in pausal form, dissolving sandhi and analyzing compounds.
history: Traditionally ascribed to Śākalya, the Padapāṭha was devised to safeguard precise word division and accentuation in oral transmission and serves as the foundation for the Ṛkprātiśākhya and Pāṇinian grammatical analysis. Maṇḍala 5 is one of the older Ṛgvedic family books.

title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 6
author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 6 attributed to the Bharadvāja family
date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
summary: The word-for-word Padapāṭha text of Maṇḍala 6 of the Ṛgveda, the 'family book' of the Bharadvāja clan. Its hymns are devoted especially to Agni and Indra, with hymns to Pūṣan and other deities; the Padapāṭha gives each word separately in its uncombined pausal form to preserve exact pronunciation and accent.
history: The Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya and is the analytical substrate for later phonetic and grammatical science. Maṇḍala 6 belongs to the archaic core of family books transmitted with remarkable oral fidelity.

title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 7
author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 7 attributed to the Vasiṣṭha family
date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
summary: The Padapāṭha (word-for-word) recitational text of Maṇḍala 7 of the Ṛgveda, the 'family book' of the Vasiṣṭha clan. It contains celebrated hymns to Agni, Indra, Mitra-Varuṇa, the goddess of dawn Uṣas, and the famous frog hymn and the maṇḍūka, and references to the Battle of the Ten Kings (dāśarājña); the Padapāṭha resolves sandhi and presents each word in isolated form.
history: The Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya. Maṇḍala 7, associated with Vasiṣṭha, is a key family book whose internal allusions are central to reconstructing early Vedic history and chronology; it was transmitted orally with extreme precision.

title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 8
author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns of Maṇḍala 8 attributed largely to the Kaṇva and Āṅgirasa families
date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1000 BCE (partly later than the family books); Padapāṭha analysis c. 7th-5th c. BCE
summary: The word-for-word Padapāṭha text of Maṇḍala 8 of the Ṛgveda, associated chiefly with the Kaṇva and Āṅgirasa poets. It includes many hymns to Indra and Agni and the distinctive Vālakhilya hymns (apocryphal supplementary hymns), and is noted for its use of the longer strophic metres; the Padapāṭha gives each word in isolated, sandhi-free pausal form.
history: The Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya. Maṇḍala 8, together with Maṇḍala 1, is generally held to be somewhat younger than the core family books (2-7), and the Vālakhilya hymns it contains are regarded as a later interpolated collection.

title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 9
author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns by many families
date_estimate: Hymns c. 1500-1200 BCE; Padapāṭha analysis later, c. 7th-5th c. BCE (Śākalya)
summary: The Padapāṭha (word-for-word) recitational text of Maṇḍala 9 of the Ṛgveda, which is unique in being entirely dedicated to Soma Pavamāna (Soma 'self-purifying' as it is filtered in the ritual). Its hymns, drawn from poets of many families, accompany the pressing and clarifying of the Soma juice; the Padapāṭha isolates each word in pausal form.
history: The Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya. Maṇḍala 9 was compiled thematically (by deity) rather than by family, gathering Soma hymns from across the other maṇḍalas, and it is central to understanding the Soma cult and the chants later elaborated in the Sāmaveda.

title: Ṛgveda-Saṃhitā: Padapāṭha text: Maṇḍala 10
author: anonymous/traditional (Padapāṭha attributed to Śākalya); hymns by many poets
date_estimate: Hymns c. 1200-1000 BCE (the latest layer of the Ṛgveda); Padapāṭha analysis c. 7th-5th c. BCE
summary: The word-for-word Padapāṭha text of Maṇḍala 10, the final and generally latest book of the Ṛgveda. It contains many of the most philosophically and literarily famous hymns, including the Puruṣasūkta (10.90), the Nāsadīya creation hymn (10.129), the Yama-Yamī and Purūravas-Urvaśī dialogue hymns, marriage and funeral hymns, and Vāc; the Padapāṭha presents each word in isolated pausal form.
history: The Padapāṭha is traditionally ascribed to Śākalya. Maṇḍala 10 is widely regarded by scholars as the youngest stratum of the Ṛgveda, linguistically and conceptually closer to the later Vedic period, and its speculative hymns prefigure Upaniṣadic thought.

title: Gopatha-Brāhmaṇa
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE or later; one of the latest Brāhmaṇas
summary: The Gopatha-Brāhmaṇa is the only surviving Brāhmaṇa attached to the Atharvaveda (linked to both the Śaunaka and Paippalāda recensions). Divided into a Pūrva-bhāga and an Uttara-bhāga, it discusses the role of the Brahman priest (the priest of the Atharvaveda) in the sacrifice, cosmogonic and mystical speculation (including praise of Om and the gāyatrī), and ritual procedure, partly drawing on and exalting the Atharvaveda's status as the fourth Veda.
history: Bloomfield argued the Gopatha-Brāhmaṇa is a comparatively late composition, dependent on the Vaitāna-Sūtra and other earlier texts, and produced within Śaunaka circles to give the Atharvaveda a Brāhmaṇa of its own; it was edited by Rajendralala Mitra and Harachandra Vidyabhushana (1872). It is generally counted among the youngest Brāhmaṇas.

title: Kauṣītaki-Brāhmaṇa [Śāṅkhāyana-Brāhmaṇa]
author: anonymous/traditional (associated with the sage Kauṣītaki and his school Śāṅkhāyana)
date_estimate: c. 600-400 BCE (Keith)
summary: The Kauṣītaki-Brāhmaṇa, also called the Śāṅkhāyana-Brāhmaṇa, is one of the two Brāhmaṇas of the Ṛgveda (the other being the Aitareya), associated with the Bāṣkala/Kauṣītaki school. In thirty adhyāyas (chapters) and 226 khaṇḍas it treats the fire ritual (Agnyādhāna), the Soma sacrifices (Agniṣṭoma and other Soma rites), and the duties of the Hotṛ priest, presenting its material in a more unified and systematic way than the Aitareya.
history: It is attributed to the sage Kauṣītaki and transmitted by the Śāṅkhāyana school, and Keith dated its codification to roughly 600-400 BCE. It overlaps substantially with the Aitareya-Brāhmaṇa in subject matter but is regarded as somewhat more concise and orderly; its concluding portion connects to the Kauṣītaki Āraṇyaka and Upaniṣad.

title: Pañcaviṃśabrāhmaṇa
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 1st millennium BCE (later than the Taittirīya-Brāhmaṇa); often placed c. 1000-600 BCE
summary: The Pañcaviṃśa-Brāhmaṇa ('Brāhmaṇa of twenty-five [chapters]'), also known as the Tāṇḍya-Mahābrāhmaṇa or Prauḍha-Brāhmaṇa, is the principal Brāhmaṇa of the Sāmaveda, belonging to its Kauthuma and Rāṇāyanīya schools. In twenty-five prapāṭhakas it expounds the duties of the Udgātṛ priests and the various sāman chants, the structure of Soma sacrifices (including the gavāmayana year-long sattra and ekāha, ahīna and sattra rites), and the legendary lore attached to many chants.
history: It is one of the oldest and largest Sāmaveda Brāhmaṇas and is generally placed later than the Taittirīya-Brāhmaṇa but within the older Brāhmaṇa stratum; it was translated into English by W. Caland. It preserves valuable legends (including the famous vrātya hymns) and detailed information on Sāmavedic ritual.

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 1
author: anonymous/traditional (the work as a whole is attributed to Yājñavalkya; Kāṇḍas 1-5 closely associated with him)
summary: Kāṇḍa 1 of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa ('Brāhmaṇa of a hundred paths'), the great prose commentary on the Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā of the Śukla Yajurveda. This first book (the Haviryajña/Ekapādikā kāṇḍa) expounds the New- and Full-Moon sacrifices (darśapūrṇamāsa) and other basic iṣṭi offerings, explaining the meaning, mythology, and rationale of each ritual act.
history: The Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa survives in two recensions, Mādhyandina (14 kāṇḍas) and Kāṇva (17 kāṇḍas), and is traditionally ascribed to the sage Yājñavalkya, the patron of the Vājasaneyi school; Julius Eggeling's five-volume translation (SBE) made it famous in the West. It is the largest and most important Brāhmaṇa, a major source for Vedic ritual, cosmology, and early mythology.

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 2
author: anonymous/traditional (work attributed to Yājñavalkya)
summary: Kāṇḍa 2 of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa, treating the establishment of the sacred fires (Agnyādheya), the Agnihotra (the obligatory daily fire oblation), the cāturmāsya (seasonal/four-monthly) sacrifices, and the piṇḍapitṛyajña (offerings to the ancestors). It expounds the symbolism and prescribed procedure of these foundational domestic and seasonal rites.
history: Part of the Mādhyandina Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa attributed to Yājñavalkya and the Vājasaneyi school of the Śukla Yajurveda; transmitted alongside the Kāṇva recension and translated by Eggeling. It is a primary source for the daily and seasonal Vedic sacrificial cycle.

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 4
author: anonymous/traditional (work attributed to Yājñavalkya)
summary: Kāṇḍa 4 of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa, continuing the exposition of the Soma sacrifice. It deals with further details of the Agniṣṭoma and related one-day Soma rites, the various graha (Soma cups) drawn for the gods, the soḍaśin and other chants and recitations, and the concluding rites of the Soma offering.
history: Part of the Mādhyandina recension of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa attributed to Yājñavalkya within the Vājasaneyi school of the Śukla Yajurveda; translated by Eggeling in the Sacred Books of the East. Kāṇḍas 1-5 form the first major division of the work and are especially associated with Yājñavalkya.

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 5
author: anonymous/traditional (work attributed to Yājñavalkya)
summary: Kāṇḍa 5 of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa, devoted largely to the great royal and complex Soma rites: the Vājapeya ('drink of strength') sacrifice and the Rājasūya (royal consecration). It describes the elaborate procedures, symbolism, and political-cosmological meaning of the rites by which a king is consecrated and asserts sovereignty.
history: Part of the Mādhyandina recension attributed to Yājñavalkya and the Vājasaneyi school of the Śukla Yajurveda; translated by Eggeling. These chapters are a key source for understanding early Indian kingship and royal ritual.

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 6
author: anonymous/traditional (Kāṇḍas 6-10 proclaimed by Śāṇḍilya)
summary: Kāṇḍa 6 opens the great Agnicayana ('piling of the fire altar') section of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa. It begins the exposition of the construction of the elaborate bird-shaped (falcon) fire altar, including the cosmogonic myth of Prajāpati creating the worlds and identifying himself with the year and the altar, and the symbolic correspondences of the bricks and layers.
history: Kāṇḍas 6-10 (the Agnirahasya / Śāṇḍilya kāṇḍas) are attributed in the text to the teacher Śāṇḍilya rather than Yājñavalkya, and are sometimes considered an originally distinct stratum incorporated into the work; translated by Eggeling. The Agnicayana is among the most complex and ancient surviving Vedic rituals.

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 7
author: anonymous/traditional (Kāṇḍas 6-10 proclaimed by Śāṇḍilya)
summary: Kāṇḍa 7 continues the Agnicayana (fire-altar) section of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa. It treats the preparation of the altar ground, the laying of the first layer of bricks, the placing of symbolically named bricks (such as the svayamātṛṇṇā, the naturally-perforated brick), and the rich web of cosmic and ritual correspondences associated with building the falcon-shaped altar.
history: Part of the Śāṇḍilya (Kāṇḍas 6-10) Agnicayana portion of the Mādhyandina recension, sometimes regarded as a separate compositional layer; translated by Eggeling. The detailed brick symbolism is a major source for Vedic cosmology and proto-geometry (śulba).

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 8
author: anonymous/traditional (Kāṇḍas 6-10 proclaimed by Śāṇḍilya)
summary: Kāṇḍa 8 continues the Agnicayana section, describing the laying of the further layers of the fire altar and the placing of additional sets of bricks (including the aśvinī, the ṛtavyā 'seasonal' bricks, and others) with their accompanying formulas. It elaborates the identification of the altar with Prajāpati, the year, the worlds, and the sacrificer himself.
history: Part of the Śāṇḍilya Agnicayana portion (Kāṇḍas 6-10) of the Mādhyandina Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa; translated by Eggeling. The intricate brick-and-cosmos symbolism of these chapters is central to the study of Vedic ritual thought.

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 9
author: anonymous/traditional (Kāṇḍas 6-10 proclaimed by Śāṇḍilya)
summary: Kāṇḍa 9 completes the building of the fire altar in the Agnicayana and describes the consecration of the finished altar: the Śatarudriya litany to Rudra, the anointing and kindling of the new fire, and the concluding offerings. It explains the symbolism of completing and vivifying the altar as the embodiment of Agni-Prajāpati.
history: Concluding portion of the Śāṇḍilya Agnicayana section (Kāṇḍas 6-10) of the Mādhyandina recension; translated by Eggeling. The Śatarudriya here is an important early hymn to Rudra-Śiva.

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 10
author: anonymous/traditional (Kāṇḍas 6-10 proclaimed by Śāṇḍilya)
summary: Kāṇḍa 10, the Agnirahasya ('secret/mystery of the fire'), gives the esoteric and speculative interpretation of the Agnicayana. It expounds the hidden symbolic meaning of the fire altar, the identification of Agni with the year, time, and Prajāpati, the building of the altar as the building up of the sacrificer's immortal self, and ideas of death and immortality that anticipate Upaniṣadic thought.
history: The final and most mystical book of the Śāṇḍilya Agnicayana section (Kāṇḍas 6-10) of the Mādhyandina recension; translated by Eggeling. Its speculative content forms a bridge from ritual Brāhmaṇa exegesis toward the philosophy of the Āraṇyakas and Upaniṣads.
